modules.title: Introduction to Open Source
modules.description: Learn what open source is, why it matters, and clarify common misconceptions.

lessons.title: What is Open Source?
lessons.description: Understand the core philosophy of open source and collaboration.

quizzes.question: What is the primary defining characteristic of open-source software?
options: It is always completely free of charge.
Its source code is publicly accessible, allowing anyone to view, modify, and distribute it.
It can only be developed by large non-profit foundations.
It has no copyright licenses associated with it.
quizzes.explanation: Open-source software is defined by its source code being publicly available for anyone to inspect, modify, and enhance under open licenses.

lessons.title: Why Open Source Matters
lessons.description: Explore the societal and technical impacts of open source projects.

quizzes.question: Which of these is a benefit of open-source collaboration for developers?
options: Guaranteed immediate high salary.
It prevents other developers from seeing your code mistakes.
Peer review improves security, quality, and helps you learn from others.
You get exclusive ownership of the shared code.
quizzes.explanation: Collaborating openly exposes code to community reviews, improving quality, security, and accelerating learning.

lessons.title: History of Open Source
lessons.description: Discover the roots of the Free Software Movement and the transition to Open Source.

quizzes.question: Who founded the Free Software Foundation (FSF) and spearheaded the GNU Project?
options: Linus Torvalds
Richard Stallman
Bill Gates
Ada Lovelace
quizzes.explanation: Richard Stallman launched the GNU project in 1983 and founded the FSF in 1985 to promote computer user freedom.

lessons.title: Benefits of Contributing
lessons.description: How contributing to open source accelerates your personal and professional growth.

quizzes.question: Which is the most common professional benefit of maintaining a public open-source portfolio?
options: It counts as official academic credit globally.
It shows employers real-world collaboration, code quality, and communication skills.
It automatically makes you immune to tech layoffs.
It grants you a license to bypass standard coding job interviews.
quizzes.explanation: A public contribution history demonstrates collaboration, coding capability, and active participation in community review processes.

lessons.title: Common Misconceptions
lessons.description: Debunking myths about code perfection and contribution types in open source.

quizzes.question: Is open source contribution limited only to writing code?
options: Yes, non-code contributions are deleted by maintainers.
No, you can contribute through documentation, design, issue reviews, translations, and community management.
Yes, only pull requests containing backend files are counted.
No, but writing code is the only way to earn badges.
quizzes.explanation: Open source relies heavily on documentation, translations, design, bug triage, and mentoring, which are highly valued non-code contributions.

modules.title: Git Fundamentals
modules.description: Master local version control, staging, commits, and local branches.

lessons.title: Repositories & Commits
lessons.description: How Git tracks project state. Initialize a repository.
lessons.hint: Run the command to initialize a repository: git init

lessons.title: Branches
lessons.description: Isolating code paths using local branches.
lessons.hint: Create a branch named feat/add-readme and switch to it using: git switch -c feat/add-readme

lessons.title: Merging
lessons.description: Integrating branch histories back together.
lessons.hint: Merge the feat/add-readme branch into your current branch: git merge feat/add-readme

lessons.title: Remotes
lessons.description: Connecting your local workspace to remote hosting services.
lessons.hint: List registered remote repositories to view URLs: git remote -v

lessons.title: Staging & Reviewing Status
lessons.description: Daily developer routine: check, stage, and commit.
lessons.hint: Type the status command to view modified files: git status

modules.title: GitHub Fundamentals
modules.description: Navigate issues, fork repos, create pull requests, and organize code.

lessons.title: GitHub Repositories
lessons.description: Understand hosting, forks, stars, and pull request tabs.

quizzes.question: What is the primary difference between a local Git repository and a GitHub repository?
options: Local Git repositories are only for private projects; GitHub is only for commercial teams.
Local Git is version control on your computer; GitHub is a cloud hosting platform with collaboration features.
GitHub doesn't track commits or branches.
Git is paid software, while GitHub is completely open-source.
quizzes.explanation: Git is the local command-line tool, whereas GitHub is a cloud-based web application facilitating shared repository management and social coding.

lessons.title: Forks
lessons.description: Creating your own copy of an upstream repository to build features.

quizzes.question: Why do open source contributors fork a repository instead of directly cloning it?
options: Direct cloning is blocked by GitHub for security.
Forking copies it to your account, giving you write access to submit your pull request.
Forking deletes the original repository for security.
Forking translates the repository's code into your native language.
quizzes.explanation: Forking creates a personal copy of the project under your GitHub profile, allowing you to push branches and request reviews on the parent project.

lessons.title: Pull Requests
lessons.description: Proposing changes to a main branch for code review.

quizzes.question: What is a Pull Request (PR) on GitHub?
options: A request to download a repository to your computer.
A formal proposal to merge code from one branch (often in a fork) to another repository's branch.
A command to force-push code to the main server.
A request to delete obsolete issues.
quizzes.explanation: A pull request is a request to pull changes from your feature branch into the target repository's code base.

lessons.title: GitHub Issues
lessons.description: Reporting bugs, requesting features, and discussing repository state.

quizzes.question: How are GitHub Issues used in open source projects?
options: They are only for reporting security vulnerabilities.
They track bugs, request features, host task discussions, and guide project boards.
They represent lines of code that contain syntax errors.
They are personal notes that only administrators can see.
quizzes.explanation: GitHub Issues are the primary tracking mechanism for tasks, feature discussions, bug reports, and planning checklists.

lessons.title: GitHub Discussions
lessons.description: Participating in Q&A, community forums, and general chats.

quizzes.question: What makes GitHub Discussions different from GitHub Issues?
options: Discussions allow you to run backend code directly.
Discussions are for open-ended community chat, ideas, and questions, while Issues track concrete work tasks.
Discussions require special licensing keys to access.
Discussions are automatically resolved after 24 hours.
quizzes.explanation: Discussions offer a forum-like space for open conversation, support, and brainstorming, keeping Issues focused strictly on project development tasks.

lessons.title: GitHub Organizations
lessons.description: Working with repositories under team structures.

quizzes.question: What is a GitHub Organization?
options: A company that writes private code only.
A shared account that groups people and repositories under a single collaborative entity.
A local folder on your computer that organizes Git branches.
A standard that formats pull request descriptions.
quizzes.explanation: Organizations enable multiple people to manage shared projects and repositories together with fine-grained access permissions.

modules.title: Open Source Etiquette
modules.description: Unwritten rules of professional communication and respect in open source communities.

lessons.title: Respect & Communication
lessons.description: How to address maintainers and write professional comments.

quizzes.question: What is the best way to handle communication with open source maintainers?
options: Tag them multiple times daily to force them to review your PR immediately.
Be polite, patient, constructive, and respect that most maintainers are volunteers working async.
Demand that they merge your code immediately.
Send private emails to their personal addresses.
quizzes.explanation: Open-source communication must be respectful, patient, and professional. Most maintainers contribute in their spare time and manage high loads.

lessons.title: Reading Guidelines First
lessons.description: The importance of checking README, CONTRIBUTING, and Code of Conduct documents.

quizzes.question: Where should you look to understand a project's setup and guidelines before opening a pull request?
options: Directly message the creator on Twitter.
Open a blank issue asking how to run the project.
Read the README.md and CONTRIBUTING.md files in the repository root.
Search general forums for random setup tutorials.
quizzes.explanation: README.md explains what the project does and how to set it up, while CONTRIBUTING.md details specific branch rules, PR requirements, and styles.

lessons.title: Professional Submissions
lessons.description: Avoid duplicate issues, spam PRs, and working without issue assignment.

quizzes.question: Which of the following actions is considered poor etiquette in open source?
options: Creating issues with detailed step-by-step reproduction guidelines.
Starting work on an issue without being assigned or communicating with the maintainers.
Linking your PR to a related open issue in the description.
Suggesting improvements to a layout's accessibility.
quizzes.explanation: Working without assignment or coordination can lead to duplicate effort and waste resources. Always ask to be assigned and outline your approach first.

lessons.title: Review & Revision Habits
lessons.description: Understand how code reviews work and how to handle revision requests.

quizzes.question: How should you respond to critical feedback on your pull request?
options: Delete the PR immediately and close your GitHub account.
Argue that your code is perfect and the maintainer doesn't understand.
Review the comments constructively, apply necessary changes, and request another review.
Submit duplicate PRs in hopes of avoiding review.
quizzes.explanation: Code review is a standard collaborative practice. Revisions are normal and help align code quality with project architecture.

modules.title: First Contribution Walkthrough
modules.description: A step-by-step interactive command line practice for setting up your first PR.

lessons.title: Submit a Mock PR
lessons.description: Walk through cloning, branching, and pushing a commit to mock origin.
lessons.hint: Set upstream and push the branch feat/my-first-pr: git push -u origin feat/my-first-pr

modules.title: Real Contribution Workflow
modules.description: Trace the step-by-step lifecycle of a professional contribution.

lessons.title: The PR Lifecycle
lessons.description: From finding an issue to getting the PR merged.

quizzes.question: Arrange the standard open source contribution workflow stages in order:
options: PR -> Issue -> Coding -> Merge -> Code Review
Issue -> Assignment -> Branch -> Development -> PR -> Code Review -> Merge
Coding -> PR -> Issues -> Merge -> Discuss
Assignment -> PR -> Merge -> Create Branch -> Test
quizzes.explanation: The standard sequence ensures consensus on the issue and proper review before code modification is integrated.

modules.title: Advanced Open Source
modules.description: Syncing forks, rebasing, squashing, conflict resolution, and CI/CD checks.

lessons.title: Rebasing & Squashing
lessons.description: Keeping commit logs clean and history linear.
lessons.hint: Rebase your current branch onto main: git rebase main

lessons.title: Conflict Resolution
lessons.description: Fixing code overlaps and continuing the rebase process.

lessons.title: CI/CD and Check Lists
lessons.description: Understanding test checks, lints, and pull request requirements.

quizzes.question: What is the purpose of CI/CD checks on GitHub pull requests?
options: They block community PRs to restrict outside edits.
They automatically run tests and lints to ensure PR code does not break the project.
They automatically merge all code changes immediately.
They delete branches that are more than 2 days old.
quizzes.explanation: Continuous Integration (CI) runs automated scripts (tests, styling checks) to guarantee new submissions adhere to quality guidelines.

modules.title: Finding Projects
modules.description: Locating open source repositories and understanding labels.

lessons.title: Good First Issues
lessons.description: Using search filters, discovering projects, and participating in Hacktoberfest.

quizzes.question: Which GitHub labels are most suitable for newcomers seeking their first contribution?
options: critical-bug, security-alert, blocks-release
good-first-issue, first-timers-only, beginner-friendly
wontfix, duplicate, invalid
documentation-only, backend-refactor
quizzes.explanation: Maintainers tag newcomer-friendly tasks with 'good first issue' or 'first-timers-only' to indicate they are self-contained and require minimal context.

modules.title: Interactive Python Sandboxes
modules.description: Learn to code using embedded Python Sandboxes in the browser.

lessons.title: Hello, Python
lessons.description: Write your first Python program.

pythonExercise.prompt: Write a Python script that defines a function `add` that returns the sum of two numbers.
pythonExercise.testCode:
assert add(2, 3) == 5, 'add(2, 3) should return 5'
assert add(-1, 1) == 0, 'add(-1, 1) should return 0'
print('Tests passed!')
pythonExercise.hint: Use the return keyword to send back the sum.

modules.title: Interactive Rust Sandbox
modules.description: Learn to code using the Rust Sandbox with syntax highlighting.

lessons.title: Hello, Rust
lessons.description: Write your first Rust program.

rustExercise.prompt: Write a Rust function `add` that returns the sum of two i32 integers.
rustExercise.hint: In Rust, the last expression in a function is returned implicitly. Omit the semicolon to return the value.

modules.title: Interactive JS/TS Sandbox
modules.description: Learn to code using embedded JavaScript/TypeScript Sandboxes in the browser.

lessons.title: Hello, JavaScript
lessons.description: Write your first JavaScript program using Web Workers.

jsExercise.prompt: Write a JavaScript function `multiply` that returns the product of two numbers. Log the result of `multiply(4, 5)` to the console.
jsExercise.testCode:
if (typeof multiply !== 'function') throw new Error('multiply function is not defined');
if (multiply(2, 3) !== 6) throw new Error('multiply(2, 3) should return 6');
jsExercise.hint: Use the return keyword to send back the product (a * b).

modules.title: Interactive Code Debugging
modules.description: Learn to step through execution, inspect variables, and track call stacks in real time.

lessons.title: Interactive Debugger
lessons.description: Set a breakpoint and step through a loop.

debugExercise.prompt: Set a breakpoint on line 4, then step through the loop to watch `sum` increase.
debugExercise.starterCode:
def calculate_sum(n):
    total = 0
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        total += i
        print(f'Added {i}, total is now {total}')
    return total

result = calculate_sum(5)
print(f'Final result: {result}')
debugExercise.hint: Click the margin next to line 4 to add a breakpoint, then click Start Debugging.
